coredata.dc:description: Mercury ion (Hg(II)) is one of the most toxic heavy metals in the environment and has seriously damaged human health. Based on this, we designed a novel fluorescence-enhanced carbon dots/mesoporous silica fluorescent composite (CDs/MSNs) fluorescent composite sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in environmental and biological systems. Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most promising carbon-based nanomaterials of the new generation, and we chose them as template molecules, and CDs were embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) by hydrothermal method to form CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). Notably, the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has a yellow luminescence with a photoluminescence intensity several times higher than the original one, and have high stability, excellent selectivity, and good biocompatibility, which can realize the selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in the environment, with a limit of detection as low as 0.89 μM. The material can also be used as an effective luminescent biological probe for the detection of Hg(II) in cells. and these properties provide long-term practical applications of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite in the fields of biological detection.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of fluorescent composite sensor based on carbon dots/ mesoporous silica nanospheres for sensitive detection of Hg(II) in cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun 130012, P. R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xirui Miao and Junmei Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ion (Hg(II)) is one of the most toxic heavy metals in the environment and has seriously damaged human health. Based on this, we designed a novel fluorescence-enhanced carbon dots/mesoporous silica fluorescent composite (CDs/MSNs) fluorescent composite sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in environmental and biological systems. Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most promising carbon-based nanomaterials of the new generation, and we chose them as template molecules, and CDs were embedded in mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) by hydrothermal method to form CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE). Notably, the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has a yellow luminescence with a photoluminescence intensity several times higher than the original one, and have high stability, excellent selectivity, and good biocompatibility, which can realize the selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in the environment, with a limit of detection as low as 0.89 μM. The material can also be used as an effective luminescent biological probe for the detection of Hg(II) in cells. and these properties provide long-term practical applications of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite in the fields of biological detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals are known to be non-negligible environmental pollutants with their toxicity, long atmospheric lifetime and ability to accumulate in the human body through bioaccumulation. Pollution of terrestrial and aquatic ecosystems by toxic heavy metals is a major environmental problem that can have irreversible effects on human health [1]. Mercury ion (Hg(II)) is one of the most toxic heavy metals in the environment and can cause serious damage to human health [2]. Mercury has elemental, organic and inorganic distribution in the environment. These toxic substances are highly persistent, toxic, and widely contaminated in soil, water, and air resources [3]. Accumulation of Hg(II) in plants amplifies the traces of the toxic element in the connecting food chain, leading to human exposure to mercury and potential genotoxicity, neurotoxicity, and carcinogenicity [4]. Hg(II) is highly bioaccumulative, and through inhalation or leakage into the body, it readily binds to sulfhydryl groups in enzymes and proteins Hg(II) is highly bioaccumulative and can easily bind to sulfhydryl groups in enzymes and proteins through inhalation or penetration into the human body, causing cellular dysfunction and irreversible damage to the human brain, nervous system and kidney function [5,6]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop sensing materials that can sensitively, rapidly and accurately detect Hg(II) in biological and environmental systems. So far, the traditional methods for detecting Hg (II) include atomic absorption spectroscopy, chromatography [7], extraction [8], etc. However, traditional methods always require expensive equipment and cumbersome processing. Many materials have also been proved to be used as sensors for Hg (II) detection, such as diamond nanoparticles [9], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [10], and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [11].

With the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, a number of novel fluorescent nanomaterials have been widely used for biological imaging and sensitive detection of metal ions [12]. Carbon dots (CDs) as a new generation of carbon materials, these quasi-spherical 0-dimensional nanoparticles, which have attracted much attention since they were first discovered during the purification of single-walled nanotubes in 2004 [13], are considered to be one of the most promising carbon-based nanomaterials of the 21st century [14]. CDs, with their excellent biocompatibility, attractive catalytic properties, excellent optical properties, and invaluable environmental friendliness, they are emerging as a new paradigm for designing light-emitting devices and show great potential in applications such as biomedical sensor, optical and photonic devices [15–17]. CDs have been realized as novel fluorescent sensor for sensing and biological imaging of important metal ions in biology, environment and industry [18,19]. Ma et al. synthesized a novel ratio fluorescent sensor based on CDs for qualitative and semi-quantitative detection of Hg(II) by complexing Rhodamine B hydrazide bonds onto the surface of CDs via an amide reaction [20]. Meng et al. synthesized fluorescent probes with bright blue emission by a one-step hydrothermal method, which showed excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of Hg(II) and could also be used as biological imaging agents for MCF-7 cell labeling [21].

In recent years, the complexation of CDs with other materials, such as polysaccharides [22], clays [23], polymers [24], and silica [25], has attracted much attention. Among them, the application of mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) is ubiquitous in various sciences. The unique characteristics of MSNs, including good biocompatibility, large specific surface area, structural controllability, pore design capability, pore size tunability, nanoparticle size distribution modulation, and the ability to generate a variety of functional groups on their surfaces, have led to the use of various types of MSNs as a unique drug delivery system [26–28]. Thus, CDs embedded in mesoporous materials can play the role of fluorescent labeling with high chemical and photoluminescence stability, low production cost and low toxicity, making them a perfect fluorescent sensing material [29]. Bhogal et al. used a molecularly imprinted fluorescent sensor constructed as a mesoporous structured silica imprint layer on the surface of CDs for selective detection of triclosan [30]. Wang et al. easily doped dispersed fluorescent CDs into mesoporous silica nanospheres for pH-triggered drug delivery and imaging [31].

Herein, we designed a novel fluorescence-enhanced sensor based on CDs for the rapid and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in environmental and biological systems. Firstly, mesoporous silica nanospheres (MSNs) were synthesized using a “surfactant-directed alkaline etching” strategy [32]. Then, CDs were selected as model molecules and embedded into MSNs by hydrothermal method to form carbon dots/mesoporous silica fluorescent composite (CDs/MSNs) functionalized with aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) luminescence. The synthesis process of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite and their response to Hg(II) are shown schematically in Scheme 1 . The fabricated CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has several times stronger yellow light emission than the original and good biocompatibility. Importantly, this material can realize the selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) with a detection limit of 0.89 μM. The material can also be used as an effective luminescent biological probe for the detection of intracellular Hg(II), which implies that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has a long-term practical application in the fields of food detection, and medical diagnosis.

O-phenylenediamine (O-PD) and was purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory, DL-Thioctic acid was obtained from Sigma-Aladdin (China), Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and Aqueous ammonia (NH3•H2O) were from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co. All reagents were analytically pure and could be used without further purification. Ultra-pure water obtained from Milli-Q purification system (Merck, Germany) was used for all experiments.

UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Japan). TEM images were obtained using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. A SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to study the internal structure of CDs/MSNs. A Nicolet IS50 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to analyze the surface functional groups of CDs/MSNs. N2 adsorption and desorption curves were obtained using a TriStar II Plus Specific Surface and Pore Size Analyzer. Cell imaging images were obtained by laser confocal scanning microscopy.

First, 14 mL of TEOS was rapidly added to a mixed solution consisting of 42.8 mL of ethanol, 6.4 mL of ammonia, and 20 mL of deionized water, which was magnetically stirred for 4 h at 30 °C. Then 10 mL of TEOS and 4 mL of KH-550 mixed solution were added drop by drop into the above mixture, and after continuing magnetic stirring for 4 h, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the silica microsphere particles (SiO2) were obtained by repeated washing with deionized water for three times. Next, sSiO2 was etched with the same volume of 0.6 M Na2CO3 aqueous solution, and condensation reflux mixing at 80 °C for 4 h. The resulting product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol for several times before the product was dried at 60 °C. Finally, the product was placed in a muffle furnace and calcined at 550 °C for 6 h to obtain the final product MSNs.

Carbon dots/mesoporous silica fluorescent composite (CDs/MSNs) was prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, 50 mg of MSNs, 108 mg of O-PD and 38 mg of DL-Thioctic acid were weighed separately and added to 10 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 20 min to make a homogeneous mixture, and the solution was transferred to a 40 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined (PTFE) stainless steel high-pressure reactor, and then taken out of the reaction for 6 h in an oven at 200 °C and naturally cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was removed to obtain an orange-yellow precipitate that was the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite. The resulting precipitate was freeze-dried to obtain a solid powder, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference detection of Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the selective response of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite to different metal ions, standard solutions containing Ag(I), Na(I), Cu(II), Mg(II), Ca(II), Fe(II), Co(II), Mn(II), K(I), Ni(II), Hg(II), Fe(II), Cr(II), Cr(VI) Al(III), Pb(II), Zn(II), Li(I)and other ions with the same concentration (10,000 μM) were prepared, respectively. 100 μL of the above different metal ion solutions were added to 3 mL of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite solution, respectively, and their fluorescence responses were recorded at 565 nm under the excitation of 410 nm excitation wavelength. Then, the anti-interference response of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite for different metal ions was investigated. The same concentration of Hg(II) was added to the mixed solution of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite containing different metal ions, and the fluorescence spectra at 565 nm were collected again to record the changes in fluorescence intensity.

To investigate the sensitivity response of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite to Hg(II). A series of Hg(II) ion solutions with different concentrations (0–10,000 μM) were configured and mixed with CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite solutions, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded under 410 nm excitation. The fluorescence spectrophotometer excitation and emission slits for all of the above and next fluorescence tests were 5.0/5.0 nm.

The HeLa cells were placed in Dalberg's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin, and incubated in an incubator at 37 °C with 5 % carbon dioxide (CO2) for 24 h. After cell culture, the cells were incubated for 24 h. After the cells were incubated, they were replaced with 100 μL of fluorescent composite of different concentrations of CDs/MSNs (250, 200, 150, 100, 50, and 0 μg/mL) of medium was replaced and incubation was continued for 24 h. Then 20 μL of MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) solution at a concentration of 5 mg/L was added and incubated for 4 h. Next, the medium containing MTT was removed and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well and shaken for 10 min at room temperature. Finally, the optical density (OD) of the mixture was measured at 490 nm and cell survival was calculated according to the formula.
Cell viability
%
=
OD Treated
/
OD Control
× 100 %
where ODControl is acquired in the absence of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, and ODTreated is obtained in the presence of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa cells were cultured in sterile 6-compartment cell culture plates in an incubator containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. 1.5 mg/mL of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite was added to each well and incubation was continued under the same conditions for 12 h. The wells were then rinsed with phosphate buffered salt solution (PBS) to remove excess CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite. Subsequently, the HeLa cells stained with CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite were incubated in the absence of Hg(II) solution and in the presence of 20 μM concentration of Hg(II) solution for 30 min, respectively. Then, the HeLa cells were fluorescently imaged using a laser confocal scanning microscope at 410 nm excitation wavelength.

We investigated the surface morphology of CDs/MSNs and fluorescent composite using TEM and SEM. The TEM of Fig. 1(a) shows that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is spherical in shape and have good dispersion in water. This phenomenon is also shown in Fig. 1(b-d), the surface of sSiO2 before alkali corrosion is smooth, while the surface of MSNs after alkali corrosion is rough there exist a large number of nano-channels, which provide a place for spatial confinement of CDs, Fig. 1(d) shows the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, which is also well dispersed. The particle size distribution of CDs/MSNs is as shown in figure Fig. S1(b), and the average particle size is about 235.15 nm. As shown in Fig. S1(a), the morphology of the prepared CDs is almost spherical, with good dispersion, no lattice structure and uniform particle size distribution. The size is mainly distributed between 1.6 nm–3.0 nm, and the average particle size is about 2.27 nm.

We performed X-ray energy spectroscopy (EDS) comparison of CDs and CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, and the results are shown in Fig. S2 CDs contain a large amount of C, N, O, and S elements, and the elements of C, N, O, S, and Si are present in large amounts in CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite. The mesoporous structures of MSNs and CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite was investigated using N2 adsorption and desorption experiments. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the MSNs exhibited a typical type IV isothermal curve and an obvious adsorption hysteresis loop, and the MSNs had a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of 45.07 m2/g and a pore volume of 0.0105 cm3/g. As shown in Fig. 2(b), when CDs were loaded into the MSNs, the isotherm of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite showed a decrease in N2 absorption, which was due to the decrease in specific surface area (5.73 m2/g) and pore volume (0.0008 cm3/g) [33]. The average pore size of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite calculated by typical hybridized nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) method was 5.62 nm, which was reduced with respect to the average pore size of MSNs of 9.38 nm, which also confirmed the immobilization of CDs in MSNs [34].

To study the elemental composition of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, we utilized the FT-IR spectrometer test to obtain the infrared spectrograms of CDs, MSNs, and CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the FT-IR spectra have several characteristic absorption peaks of SiO2, in which the strong peak at 1112 cm−1 is the asymmetric stretching vibration of SiO, the symmetric stretching vibration at 804 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric stretching vibration of SiO, the sharp peak at 473 cm−1 is the bending vibration of Si-O-Si, and the broad vibrational peaks at 3440 cm−1 is the water Si-OH and OH stretching vibration peaks in water, and the vibration peaks at 1518 cm−1-1685 cm−1 originated from the amide groups in the CDs, and the FT-IR spectra of the CDs are shown in Fig. S3, confirming the successful construction of the fluorescent composite of CDs/MSNs [29,35,36]. The position and intensity of the measured diffraction peaks were used to judge the structure and crystalline state, as shown in Fig. 2(d), there is a broad peak at 2θ = 23° and no other characteristic diffraction peaks, and the XRD spectra indicate the amorphous nature of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite [37]. The chemical compositions of CDs/MSNs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S4(a), there are five typical peaks at 533 eV (O1s), 400 eV (N1s), 285 eV (C1s),164 eV(S2p) and 103 eV(Si2p) in the XPS, suggesting that our prepared CDs/MSNs are consisted of O, N, C, S and Si elements. Fig. S4(b) C1s shows four different characteristic peaks at 284.80 eV (C − C), 285.46 eV (C − N), 288.95 eV (O-C=O) and 288.31 eV (C=O) [38,39]. For the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s on Fig. S4(c), two different peaks at 399.14 eV (Pyridinic N) and 400.04 eV (Amino N) can be observed [40]. Fig. S4(d) shows that the O1s region is divided into two peaks corresponding to 532.81 eV (C=O) and 531.01 eV (CO) [41]. The S2p in Fig. S4(e) is 163.42 eV and 164.61 eV, which are related to SC and SO [42], respectively. As shown in Fig. S4(f), the Si2p spectra peak at 103.06 and 103.70 eV, assigned to the Si-O-C/Si-O and Si-O-Si [43,44], consistent with the FT-IR distribution. Overall, the combination of TEM, BET, FT-IR, XRD and XPS analyses indicated the successful synthesis of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite.

Subsequently, in order to study the composite mechanism of CDs/MSNs, we measured the Zeta potential of CDs, MSNs and CDs/MSNs. As shown in Fig. S5, the surface of CDs had a positive charge of 4.68 mV, while MSNs had a potential of −15.6 mV due to the presence of silicon hydroxyl (Si-OH), and CDs/MSNs had a negative charge of −9.34 mV after composite, which proved that CDs/MSNs were formed by electrostatic assembly between CDs and MSNs.

In order to optimize the experiments, we changed different experimental conditions as a way to obtain CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite with more excellent fluorescence properties. With other experimental conditions remaining unchanged, the hydrothermal reaction time was changed, as shown in Fig. S6, with the increase of reaction time, the fluorescence intensity firstly increased and then gradually decreased, and the optimal hydrothermal reaction time was 6 h. With other experimental conditions remaining unchanged, the reaction temperature was changed, as shown in Fig. S7, with the continuous increase of the reaction temperature, the fluorescence intensity showed an irregular change, and the optimal reaction temperature was 200 °C. The optimal reaction conditions we determined here were all used for subsequent experiments.

To further investigate the fluorescence properties of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, we performed fluorescence analysis, UV–vis absorption analysis, and stability analysis of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite in aqueous solution. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs/MSNs in aqueous solution obtained using different excitation wavelengths from 350 nm- 440 nm. From Fig. 3(a), it can be observed that CDs/MSNs have a wide range of excitation wavelengths, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs is gradually enhanced with the increasing excitation wavelength from 350 nm- 410 nm, whereas the fluorescence intensity decreases when the excitation wavelength is larger than 410 nm, and the position of the emission peaks is always constant, which indicates that CDs/MSNs do not have an excitation wavelength This indicates that CDs/MSNs do not have excitation wavelength dependence. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs solution also showed a similar situation, as shown in Fig. S8, with the increasing excitation wavelength, only the fluorescence intensity changed, while the shape and position of the emission peaks did not change, which confirmed that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite was mainly due to the yellow color luminescence caused by CDs [45]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the UV–Vis spectrum of CDs/MSNs solution has a broad absorption strong peak at 412 nm, which may be attributed to the n → π* jump of the C=O/C-N bond [46]. The optimum excitation wavelength of CDs/MSNs solutions is 410 nm, and the optimum emission wavelength is obtained at 565 nm, which is also the same as the position of the absorption peaks in the UV–Vis spectral graphs is the same. And from the inset of Fig. 3(b), it can be observed that the CDs/MSNs solution has a nearly transparent color under natural light and emits a bright and vivid yellow fluorescence under the irradiation of UV lamp at 365 nm.

We also compared the fluorescence emission spectra of MSNs, CDs and CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite in aqueous solution, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(a). It is noteworthy that at the same excitation wavelength of 410 nm, the MSNs solution is almost non-fluorescent, while the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is several times that of the CDs solution, showing a strong AIEE property, which may be attributed to the fact that CDs nanoparticles are aggregated in the pore space of the MSNs, which enhances the Si-O-Si bonding and CDs interactions and suppressed the vibrational leaps of CDs, which led to a great enhancement of the fluorescence intensity [47,48]. The hue of the CDs/MSN solution was quantified using the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. It can be visualized from Fig. 4(b) that the CIE 1931 coordinates (0.47, 0.54) of the CDs/MSN solution are located in the yellow region, which is consistent with the fluorescence spectroscopy results described above. It is further shown that CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite emit yellow fluorescence [49,50].

To explore the detection performance of the prepared CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite, we conducted a series of experimental studies on their stability under different conditions. In the dark, we placed the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite solution under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation for a number of times, collected the CDs/MSNs solution at every interval of 10 min and recorded the change of its fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. S9(a), with the increase of UV lamp irradiation time, the change of the relative fluorescence intensity of the CDs/MSNs solution was not obvious, which indicated that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has good UV resistance. Fig. S9(b) shows the fluorescence changes of CDs/MSNs under different concentrations of salt solutions, and it can be clearly observed that the fluorescence curves change very little with the increasing concentration of NaCl solution, indicating that the salt solution has almost no effect on the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite. We gradually changed the pH of the CDs/MSNs solution from acidic to alkaline, as shown in Fig. S9(c), and found that the relative fluorescence intensity of the solution pH was higher in the range of 6–12, indicating that the CDs/MSNs solution had good stability under weakly acidic or alkaline conditions. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs/MSNs solution was almost quenched when the solution pH was <4 or the pH was >13, which might be related to the protonation of -COOH and -OH groups on the surface of CDs [51]. After the prepared CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite was stored at room temperature for one month, as shown in Fig. S9(d), a slight enhancement of the fluorescence intensity was observed, which may be due to the fact that there are more CDs nanoparticles clustered with the pores of the MSNs, and the interaction bonds between the two are enhanced with each other [52,53], which suggests that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite can be stored for a long period of time for the detection and can ensure its detection accuracy. In summary, it is proved that CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite has good photoluminescence stability, reliability and practicality, which make them a promising fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective and interference for the detection of Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: For an excellent fluorescent sensor, not only good stability but also high sensitivity and accuracy are important. To explore the potential applications of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor, we investigated the selective detection of different metal ions by CDs/MSNs solutions. We prepared common metal ion solutions of the same concentration, including Ag(I), Na(I), Cu(II), Mg(II), Ca(II), Fe(II), Co(II), Mn(II), K(I), Ni(II), Hg(II), Fe(II), Cr(II), Cr(VI), Al(III), Pb(II), Zn(II) and Li(I). Under the same experimental conditions, different metal ion solutions were added to the CDs/MSNs solution with a blank control group. Under the excitation of 410 nm excitation wavelength, the comparative plots of the effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs solution were obtained, and it was clearly observed from Fig. 5(a) that under the same concentration and the same detection conditions, the selectivity of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite to Hg(II) was superior to that of the other metal ions, and the addition of Hg(II) caused the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs solution to The sharp quenching of the CDs/MSNs solution fluorescence intensity by the addition of Hg(II), while the addition of other common metal ions did not change the CDs/MSNs solution fluorescence intensity much, indicating that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is extremely sensitive to Hg(II), which may be attributed to the much stronger affinity of Hg(II) for the hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite than that of the other metal ions [54]. It may also be due to the fact that Hg(II) acts as an acceptor of electrons and has a better ability to absorb electrons than other metal ions [55]. Meanwhile, in order to determine the anti-interference ability of the material, we added the same concentration of Hg(II) into the mixed CDs/MSNs solutions containing different common metal ions, respectively, as shown in the bar graph of Fig. 5(b), the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite remained drastically when Hg(II) was added into the CDs/MSNs solutions containing other common metal ions quenching, which demonstrated that the coexistence of CDs/MSNs solution and other metal ions did not interfere with the detection of Hg(II), indicating that the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite had high selectivity and anti-interference for Hg(II) [56,57]. In order to explore the selectivity of CDs/MSNs to Hg(II) from CDs, we performed cation selectivity experiments and anti-interference experiments of CDs (Fig. S10). The results show that CDs have significant selectivity for Hg(II) and are not easily interfered by other cations during detection, which further confirms that the prepared CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite may be a promising fluorescent sensor.

To investigate the quenching mechanism of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor, we combined UV absorption visible spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S11, the presence of Hg(II) greatly reduced the absorption intensity at 290 nm with a blue shift, while the intensity of the absorption peak at 420 nm was similarly reduced, which may be due to the interaction of Hg(II) with CDs/MSNs, indicating a static quenching mechanism of the fluorescence of CDs/MSNs composite. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs/MSNs and CDs/MSNs+Hg(II) are 1.68 ns and 1.67 ns, respectively. The change of fluorescence lifetime was not obvious, which further confirmed that the fluorescence quenching process caused by Hg(II) was static quenching [58,59]. The quenching mechanism of the fluorescence of CDs/MSNs composite by Hg(II) could be due to two reasons. MSNs composite fluorescence quenching may be due to two reasons: firstly, the adsorption capacity of MSNs to Hg(II) is higher than other metal ions, and Hg(II) as a transition metal can easily bind to the lighter donor atoms [60]; secondly, the luminescent body of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is CDs, and due to the strong affinity of Hg-SH bonds, Hg(II) easily combines with the sulfur-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs to form complexes [61–63], which breaks the original dense structure of CDs/MSNs composite and leads to the disappearance of AIEE phenomenon. Overall, the interaction between ligands and metal ions on the surface of CDs/MSNs composite in the presence of Hg(II) leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs/MSNs composite [64].

In order to investigate the sensitivity response of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor to Hg(II). We configured Hg(II) solutions with different concentrations (0–10,000 μM), mixed them with CDs/MSNs solutions and measured the fluorescence response intensity. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs solution decreased slowly at first with the increasing concentration of Hg(II) solution, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs solution gradually decreased and nearly quenched when the concentration of Hg(II) solution increased from 200 μM to 10,000 μM. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor at an emission wavelength of 565 nm and the concentration of Hg(II) solution is shown in Fig. 6(b), and the inset shows that, in the range of 0–100 μM of the concentration of Hg(II) solution, the concentration of Hg(II) and the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent sensor (F/F0) show a good linear relationship with a linear regression equation of y = −0.00212× + 1.01775 (R2 = 0.98909), where F and F0 denote the fluorescence intensity values in the presence and absence of Hg(II) solution, respectively [65,66]. The lowest limit of detection (LOD) for the Hg(II) response was 0.89 μM
LOD =
3 σ
m
, σ represents the standard deviation of the blank, m represents the slope of the linear fitting curve), which demonstrated that the fluorescent sensor of the CDs/MSNs composite was effective for the low concentration of Hg(II) with high detection sensitivity. The sensing properties of different materials for Hg (II) detection are shown in Table S1. The detection limit and quantum yield (QY) are compared with the prepared CDs/MSNs. The results show that the CDs/MSNs prepared in this study have higher emission wavelengths and better detection sensitivity for Hg(II) compared with other methods.

To visualize and quickly observe the characteristics of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor for Hg(II) detection, we developed a low-cost, simple and sensitive Hg(II) test paper. The test paper soaked with CDs/MSNs solution was dropped with different concentrations (0–1000 μM) of Hg(II) solution, and the color change of the test paper was observed. From Fig. 7 , it can be clearly observed that the color of the test paper under natural light did not change significantly in the presence of different concentrations of Hg(II) solution, while the fluorescent color gradually changed from bright yellow to dark blue with the increase of the concentration of Hg(II) solution under the irradiation of the UV lamp at 365 nm, showing an obvious change of the color gradient, which suggests that the fluorescent test paper based on the CDs/MSNs composite may still maintain the same dispersion as the aqueous solution [67,68].

In order to verify whether the prepared CDs/MSNs can detect Hg(II) in real samples, we used Nanhu Lake and tap water as real samples (Table S2). The recovery rate was used to detect Hg(II). According to table 1, the recovery rate of Hg(II) in Changchun Nanhu Lake is between 99.73 % and 100.80 %, and the recovery rate of Hg(II) in tap water is between 100.20 % and 101.05 %. The relative standard deviation (n = 3) of Hg(II) detection can be controlled below 2.1 %, indicating that the fluorescence sensor has good accuracy and reproducibility and can be applied to the detection of Hg(II) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity assay and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the high selectivity and sensitivity of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor to Hg(II), we investigated its fluorescence response to Hg(II) in biological terms. Using Hela cells as a model, cytotoxicity was assessed in cells incubated with the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor using MTT assay prior to imaging. The activities of HeLa cells incubated under different concentrations of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite are shown in Fig. S13, and it can be clearly observed that even when the concentration of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is as high as 250 mg/L, the survival rate of Hela cells is still around 85 %, and the IC50 is 847.7 mg/L. This proves the low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and possibility in the field of biological imaging of the obtained composite materials [69,70].

To study the detection of Hg(II) in living cells, Hela cells were incubated with CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite material for 12 h, then rinsed with PBS buffer containing 20 μM of Hg(II) solution and imaged under a laser confocal scanning microscope. As shown in Fig. 8 , there is obvious yellow fluorescence when Hg(II) is not present in the cells, and when Hg(II) is present in the cells, it can be clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite decreases, which suggests that CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite can be used as an effective biological probe for the detection of Hg(II) in the living cells, and it further demonstrates that the prepared fluorescent sensor side of the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite is very biocompatible, and provides the basis of the bio-imaging, which has a great potential of development in the detection of Hg(II) in the cells [71–73].

In this paper, we designed a novel and highly stable CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor for the rapid and sensitive detection of Hg(II) in environmental and biological systems. CDs with QY of 23%were embedded into MSNs by hydrothermal method, which resulted in the AIEE phenomenon. The prepared CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor not only has excellent photoluminescence performance, high stability and low cytotoxicity, but more importantly, the sensor can realize the selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II). The QY of CDs/MSNs was as high as 31 %, with a detection limit as low as 0.89 μM. At the same time, the sensor has a good biocompatibility, and it can be used as an effective luminescent biological probe for detecting intracellular of Hg(II). In summary, the CDs/MSNs fluorescent composite sensor is expected to achieve effective application value in environmental detection, food detection, medical diagnostics, life sciences and other aspects in the future.
